item.title: Five Economic Storylines That Will Define America's Second Half of 2026
item.body:
The first half of 2026 is in the books, and the American economy enters July balanced between resilience and uncertainty. Here are the five storylines economists, investors, and households will be watching between now and December.

## 1. The Fed's next move

The Federal Reserve meets again in late July, and the central question hasn't changed: is inflation cooling fast enough to justify easier policy, or does a still-spending consumer argue for patience? Every jobs report and inflation print between now and that meeting will be read like tea leaves. Markets have repeatedly gotten ahead of the Fed over the past two years — the second half of 2026 will test whether they finally have it right.

## 2. Tariffs meet the price tag

The sweeping tariff regime built out since 2025 is no longer an abstraction — it is embedded in supply chains, retail prices, and corporate earnings calls. The key question for the second half: how much of the cost continues to be absorbed by importers and retailers, and how much lands on consumers just as holiday-season buying begins. Small businesses, with thinner margins than the giants, remain the most exposed.

## 3. The AI investment engine

Capital spending on artificial intelligence — data centers, chips, power infrastructure — has become one of the largest single contributors to U.S. economic growth. That makes the economy unusually dependent on a handful of technology giants maintaining their spending pace. Any sign of pullback in AI capex would ripple far beyond Silicon Valley, into construction, utilities, and regional labor markets.

## 4. The consumer's stamina

American consumers have defied predictions of retreat for years, but the cracks are worth watching: credit card balances near record levels, delinquencies elevated among younger borrowers, and a job market where openings are harder to land than at any point since the pandemic era. A summer of World Cup tourism and 250th-anniversary travel is giving services spending a boost — the question is what happens when the party winds down in the fall.

## 5. Midterm math

The November 3 midterm elections are now four months away, and the economy will be the ballot's silent headliner. Historically, perceptions of inflation and job security formed in the summer harden into voting behavior by fall. Expect every economic data release from here forward to be fought over as political ammunition — and expect policy proposals, from tax tweaks to tariff adjustments, to be timed with the electorate in mind.

**The bottom line:** none of these storylines resolves on its own. The Fed's path depends on tariff-driven prices; consumer stamina depends on the labor market; and the political stakes in November depend on all of it. The second half of 2026 won't be boring.
